note_data.title: The social approach
keyPoints: According to the biological approach, humans are biological organisms made up of physiological processes, so all thoughts and cognitive processes must be biological in origin
The actions of genes, hormones, neurotransmitters and neurochemical mechanisms must be understood to explain behaviour fully
Heritability coefficients quantify the genetic basis of characteristics - IQ has a coefficient of 0.5 showing equal influence of nature and nurture
An individual's genotype is their genetic make-up, while phenotype is the physical expression of their genotype
Two people may have the same genotype but different phenotypes due to personal choices or epigenetics
Natural selection is the mechanism of evolution where genetically-determined behaviours giving selective advantage are passed to future generations
Evolution is the process by which organisms change over time as a result of changes in heritable physical or behavioural traits

definitions.definition: The study of the influence of an organism's genetic composition on its behaviour and the interaction of heredity and environment insofar as they affect behaviour
An individual's genetic make-up, where a gene is a short section of DNA coding for specific proteins
The physical expression of an individual's genotype
A change in gene expression, without altering an individual's genetic make-up
The mechanism of evolution where genetically-determined behaviour giving selective advantage increases chances of surviving and reproducing

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give brief details without explanation - stick to key features only
Give detailed account including procedure, findings and conclusions for studies

modelAnswer.question: Explain two factors that affect conformity. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Group size affects conformity because conformity increases with group size up to about 4-5 people, after which it levels off. This is because a larger group creates more pressure to conform but beyond a certain point additional members have little extra influence. Task difficulty affects conformity because when tasks become more difficult or ambiguous, people are more likely to look to others for guidance about the correct response. This is because people use others as a source of information when they are uncertain about their own judgement.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Group size identified as factor
Mark 2: Explanation of how group size affects conformity
Mark 3: Task difficulty identified as factor
Mark 4: Explanation of how task difficulty affects conformity

commonMistakes: Confusing genotype and phenotype - remember genotype is genetic make-up while phenotype is physical expression
Assuming twin studies can clearly separate nature vs nurture effects when MZ twins may share more similar environments
Overlooking that biological determinism has serious implications for moral responsibility in the judicial system
examinerTips: Use specific examples like heritability coefficient of 0.5 for IQ to demonstrate understanding of genetic influence
Evaluate using biological determinism criticism - link to real-world implications for criminal justice and insurance
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Asch's line judgment task showing the standard line and three comparison lines A, B, C, where participants had to match the standard line length despite confederates giving obviously wrong answers
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how social identity theory could explain prejudice between groups
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (4 marks) — candidates had to describe the process of social facilitation with reference to Zajonc's theory
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — students needed to evaluate the social learning theory using evidence from Bandura's studies
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q12 (4 marks) — they asked for two ways that social loafing could be reduced in group work situations

workedExample.question: Explain how social identity theory can account for discrimination. Use an example in your answer. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define social identity theory - people categorize themselves into social groups which become part of their identity
Step 2: Explain in-group favoritism - people favor their own group to maintain positive self-esteem
Step 3: Explain out-group discrimination - people discriminate against other groups to enhance their group's status
Step 4: Provide a clear example - football fans showing loyalty to their team while hostility to rival supporters
Step 5: Link example back to theory - fans gain positive identity from group membership and discriminate to maintain superiority
workedExample.answer: Social identity theory suggests people categorize themselves into groups which become part of their self-concept. To maintain positive self-esteem, individuals show in-group favoritism and out-group discrimination. For example, football supporters demonstrate extreme loyalty to their team while showing hostility toward rival fans. This occurs because supporting their team enhances their social identity, while discriminating against other teams helps maintain their group's perceived superiority and their own positive self-image.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for definition of social identity theory, 2 marks for explaining in-group/out-group processes, 2 marks for appropriate example, 1 mark for linking example to theory

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused social facilitation with social loafing' — always clearly distinguish that social facilitation improves performance on simple tasks, while social loafing reduces individual effort in groups

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often described studies without explaining the social processes involved' — always link Bandura's findings back to social learning mechanisms like modeling, imitation and vicarious reinforcement

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave vague examples of prejudice' — always provide specific, detailed examples when discussing social identity theory, such as realistic conflict theory studies like Sherif's Robbers Cave experiment
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the Social Learning Theory cycle: Start with 'Model performs behavior' → 'Observer pays attention' → 'Observer retains behavior in memory' → 'Observer reproduces behavior when motivated' → 'Consequences determine future imitation'. Include arrows showing the circular nature, and label each stage with key factors (attention, retention, reproduction, motivation). Add examples like Bandura's Bobo doll study to illustrate each stage.
note_data.quickRecap: The biological approach views humans as biological organisms where all behaviour stems from physiological processes including genes, hormones and neurotransmitters. Key concepts include genotype vs phenotype, heritability coefficients, and natural selection driving evolution. The approach has practical applications in drug development but faces criticism for biological determinism and methodological issues in twin studies.
